This year marks the European Year of Intercultural Dialogue.

For this very reason, multiple initiatives associated with this year have been carried out by the different areas of action and intervention.
In this context, the Erasmus Mundus program also plays its part. And in this new perspective, the new regulation is already present.

The Erasmus Mundus program constitutes an important contribution to the creation of centers of excellence in the EU, which will, in a way, help stem the brain drain from the European Union. As well as representing a significant, a significant role in

in the promotion of European values, together with nationals from third countries who come here to study and who find in the old continent a unique cultural and linguistic diversity, which functions as an attractive hub and is distinct from existing models in the rest of the world.

The problem of visas for Erasmus students in host countries and the frequent difficulties in obtaining and renewing them often lead them to live in a semi-clandestine situation in the European Union, as they attend master's programs with tourist visas, some of which are expired.
We consider the search for a solution that allows for speed, transparency, and effectiveness in the issuance of visas for these students to be of the utmost relevance and urgency.

Also, the knowledge of the language, which allows for cultural perception and mutual coexistence, beyond the strictly academic domain, is crucial. Therefore, we must absolutely guarantee this premise.
To conclude, a word of gratitude to Deputy Dessa Arnês, for the way she conducted this entire process and for the balanced report she produced.

Mr. President, Commissioner, dear colleagues,
Should the authorization for granting state aid for the modernization and renewal of fishing fleets in the outermost regions only be possible until the end of 2009, or should it be in force for an indefinite period?
The particularities of fishing activity in these regions justify that these aids can be granted beyond 2009, without the marine environment being minimally harmed.
State aid for the modernization and renewal of the fleets of the outermost regions for an indefinite period does not mean permanent and eternal aid.
would be immediately interrupted if the studies and evaluation of the scientific council and of the regional consultative councils evidenced that need.
This is the solution that would best suit the very specific reality of the fisheries of the outermost regions.

The same reasons that underpin an exclusive fishing reserve up to 100 miles for the fishermen of each RUP also justify differentiated treatment regarding aid for the modernization and renewal of the fleet.
Fortunately, within the Fisheries Committee, the best solutions were found that can address the particularities of fisheries in the outermost regions.

Mr President, Mr Commissioner Almunia, Mr President of the Eurogroup,
I want to congratulate not only the rapporteurs, but also the Commission, for the initiative of having produced this text. The assessment of the 10 years of Economic and Monetary Union.
It is a strategic text, and this analysis was fundamental.
The euro, as was said here today, is an undeniable success.

From Lisbon to Helsinki, from Dublin to Bratislava, the Euro proved its resilience and solidity, even during the severe turbulence we witnessed. And it is clearly important that the borders of this club be expanded.
But the current times daily put to the test the solidity of the European project in many other dimensions.
The euro, as I said, is a central instrument for the functioning of the financial system.

However, the text, the Commission's own text, clearly points to the fundamental conclusion that neither the growth of the real economy nor convergence, in both social and spatial terms, accompanied that success.
On the contrary, the commission clearly concludes that the disparities worsened violently during the decade of the single currency's operation.
This divergence affects regions of my country, in particular, specifically the north of Portugal, but also other regions of member countries.
The impacts of the single monetary policy, namely due to the very high exchange rate practiced, hit more the regions that are more exposed to international competition and the most exporting regions.
Today, the crisis that resulted from the deregulation of financial markets is violently affecting the real economy. And it further aggravates the situation of many citizens and many regions.

these regions, those exposed to international competition, those dependent on small and medium-sized enterprises, those where access to credit is most important, are today becoming victims of this process and even face depression.
Mr. Commissioner, Mr. President of the Eurogroup, the success of the Euro depends on the confidence that European citizens place in it.
The most powerful European countries have already initiated processes for the relaunch of the national economy.
We have the particular case of the initiatives that concern the automotive industry.
But Europe is more than that.
It has to be much more than the more or less coordinated sum of national policies.
The time has come to transform the euro into a central tool for Europe, to support a robust currency, but also a robust real economy. And to ensure that the functioning of the euro is accompanied by,
of mechanisms that ensure the central objective of achieving social and regional convergence, because it is, it is at the essence, it is at the very core of the European project.
The debate on the coordination of economic policies in the Euro area today has a strengthened and unquestionable impact and relevance.
But coordinating national policies is not enough.
It is necessary

Having a Lisbon strategy, which is an aggregation of national initiatives, and a stability and growth pact, which differentially constrains the different countries that use it and to which they must submit, are limitations, to which is added the limited budget we face.
Crises generate opportunities.
The monetary union can only be sustained by a convergence of well-being among its member countries and their citizens, regardless of their birthplace.
What other way would we have to mobilize citizens for the single currency?

The commission must live up to its own diagnosis, and that is why we eagerly await the concrete proposals that the commission will bring to this plenary on November 26th and will present to European citizens.

It is essential that, starting from this crisis, a new phase of the Union be launched, in which the consolidation and strength of the financial system are accompanied by an effective prosperity built on cohesion and also economic citizenship.

more proposals. One only needs to look at the proposals that were timely put forward by the socialists and many of which were subsequently adopted by this parliament.

To my cabinet, and very particularly to Joana Benzinho, to everyone my very sincere thanks, in the name of a more harmonious and structured development for the coastal regions and for tourism in the European Union.
The 27 member states of the European Union collectively account for over 89,000 km of coastline.

This presents a variety of very specific characteristics which, depending on their location, are distinguished either by their cosmopolitanism, if we speak of cities like Lisbon, Copenhagen or Stockholm, or

with the difficulties of being peripheral or outermost regions, with connectivity issues to major urban centers, or with accelerated desertification contexts, such as the Algarve, Liguria, the Canary Islands, or Madeira.
In one way or another, everyone identifies with the notion of coast and coastal zone, and daily experiences the advantages and disadvantages associated with it.
According to the available data, by 2010 about 75% of humanity will live in coastal areas. These areas,
According to the circuits and their interrelationships, we define it as the 50 kilometers in a straight line that go from the coastline to the inland areas.
Yes, regions and not maritime zones detached from an integrated vision, which these matters so much lack, and which our ancestors would also endorse.
Here, all this population will concentrate in search of opportunities and economic synergies. And in many cases, with the sole expectation that these will revolve around tourism.
Thus, the evident need for a pragmatic and integrated vision of the impact of tourism on coastal areas took hold of our minds and led us to get to work.

In the current context of financial crisis, in which the impact on the real economy becomes increasingly insurmountable, tourism appears as a sector with an enormous potential to be directly or indirectly affected in a brutal way.

The regions that, for their development, have an overwhelming or total dependence on this, see their activity threatened and look to the future with distrust. Especially because tourism is not currently a competence of the European Union.
However, articulated measures can and must be taken, and the spirit in which the Lisbon Treaty was imbued reflects that same tendency.
However, to wait until the treaty enters into force to start acting is to wait for the stage when we will cry over spilled milk.
The current political and tourism activity, and the fragile reality of the regions that depend on it, demand urgent and effective action from us.

The fact that these are areas structurally dependent on tourism, as an employment promoter. Despite often being seasonal employment, this labor-intensive activity cannot be concealed in a context of pressure on urban areas and on unemployment.

For this very reason, this report that we now bring to you here, which was already of the greatest opportunity and urgency when this house's Committee on Regional Development decided to promote it, now assumes that it should be seen immediately as the priority for the European Commission and for the European Council.

Priority, yes, to the numerous initiatives that this incorporates, as well as those for which it assumes its express support, and which have already been conveyed by other institutions, alongside the measures taken in the emergency plan.

of the commission, from which I highlight the revision of the Globalization Adjustment Fund, in which the issues associated with this sector and the shock to which it is currently subjected, must, obviously, be covered.

It is crucial to ensure the development of new segments of the economy in these coastal areas, ensuring their social and environmental sustainability. And promoting the real integration of different sectoral policies, such as the maritime sector, transport,

of energy, the cohesion instruments already on the ground. The new quality product policy, which is the revision of the CAP, incorporates with the announcement of the Welcheck report and the new tourism products, in these coastal areas, taking into account their crucial contribution to the European economy.
the implementation of an adequate and holistic vision for this policy must shortly become a reality in the European Union.

Finally, and to conclude, Mr. President, only with the clear articulation of these instruments and a swift and effective action, involving all the intervening partners already on the ground, will we be able to guarantee that we will have in the European Union a sustainable coastal tourism sector with a future. Thank you very much.

where it exists, it will serve the citizen and strengthen our democracy.

Mr. President, Mr. Barroso, colleagues,
I commend the work of Carlos Coelho, Shadow Martin Rury and the other Shadows.
In December 2004, the Council adopted a regulation establishing standards for the security features and biometric data of passports and travel documents issued by the Member States.
Now, we found it necessary to take new steps to combat abduction and child trafficking.
The use of passports by children, according to the principle of one person, one passport, can be a fundamental way to win this difficult and important battle.
In my country, Portugal, the collection of fingerprints from children aged 6 and older is already a practice with a long tradition, which perhaps for this reason, causes me no objection.
As a defender of the European cause, I believe that, in this matter, it is important to have harmonization.

It reassures me to know that member states, such as Portugal, which long ago set the minimum age at six years old to proceed with the collection of fingerprints, will not have to alter their national legislation.
It should be underlined that the security of passports, which is strengthened today, is not exhausted by the existence of a passport.

The passport, in itself, represents an increase in the level of security, ranging from the presentation of the necessary documents for the issuance of passports, followed by the collection of biometric data, up to the verification and matching at border control posts.
This report is another step to affirm the rights of the individual and guarantee their safety.

industrial, for the creation of a true European market in the defense industries, which can also contribute to strengthening research and development, not only in the defense industries, but also in a set of European industries that benefit from the development of the defense industries.

The European Union, as it is, is almost a peaceful superpower. We call these industries defense industries and not war industries, because we want peace and not war, and that is also why we need defense industries.

President, Madam Commissioner. I will try to put the questions in a very concise way.

to respond to growth crises naturally requires a political response. But responding to humanitarian crises, emergency situations, and military crises, like the one experienced in late 2008, early 2009, is crucial.

The interest in the approval of this directive, of this proposed directive, essentially lies in how it can contribute to boost economic activity at the local level and support actions at the social level.

This application of a reduced rate is of great importance in the housing sector, both in construction and in renovation, allowing the Member States that adopt it to reduce the respective cost for the end-user.

The provision of local services, namely by small businesses, can also benefit from this reduction in the VAT rate, securing and generating more and better employment, through the promotion of the consumption of goods and services, thus stimulating the economy itself.
Catering activity, fundamental for the good functioning of local economies and the very increase in tourism, can thus gain a lot at the local level with this reduction in VAT taxation.
These services, dear colleagues, should not circulate in the European internal market, which will help to reduce the reserves of some member states already verified under the Council's plan.
Therefore, I cannot agree with the extension of this reduced tax on equipment, no matter the pretext.

The prudent way this proposal should be handled in the European Parliament should not give pretext to governments that have some reservations regarding the European Commission's proposal, to practically exercise the right of veto that European legislation still grants them in fiscal matters.
Thus, here, one must prudently reject alterations without much sense, which only hinder the acceptance of the text.
As has already been mentioned here, not only is the perfect the enemy of the good, but also he who wants everything, loses everything.
This proposed directive is important not only for strengthening local economies, but also corresponds to the ambitions repeatedly expressed by various Member States.
We therefore support the committee's proposal as it was presented. This was, moreover, the meaning of the vote, with my agreement, of the Committee on the Internal Market and Consumer Protection.

a word to disagree with my colleague Edith Estrela. And that's what motivated me to speak. Ah, when she caricatured the intentions of the Czech presidency.
Nobody wants to send women home. The problem is that there are women who want or would like to stay at home.
Even the associations that try to defend them. Women who are ignored, despised, discriminated against, because they want to dedicate themselves to their family, and we don't listen to them.
And this is a matter of freedom. It is a matter of freedom of choice, which the left is so fond of evoking, but which it forgets here.
It is about respecting or not the organizational freedom of couples.
It is about being able to provide to those who wish to do so more quality of life, more paternity, and more quality maternity.
That is what our society also needs to do.
We won't achieve it; we won't solve the problems of birth rate and people's happiness with prejudices.
We address them with policies tailored to reality and the spontaneous wishes of the people.
Let's not go there with an obsession with the state and the market.
Let's go with a fair look at the family.
necessary, dear colleagues. The difficult situation in Quimonda was exacerbated by the emergence of the financial and economic crisis.

that no national or state executive commits the error of allowing this company to close facilities and liquidate jobs in the territory of the Union.

to commit the Commission to supporting Qimonda, overcoming possible communication failures, is the appeal we make to Commissioner Vladimir Špidla, to whom we are bound by ties of work and political solidarity, and to President Barroso, who will never forget that he is our compatriot and that of a significant part of Qimonda's workers.

Mr. President, Mr. Commissioner,
For years we have been talking about the Lisbon Strategy. And the times we live in are perhaps not the most suitable to talk about the Lisbon Strategy, but there is no doubt that we need a strategy.
a strategy that responds to the difficulties and challenges that the present crisis poses to us.
And that is what we also expect from the commission.

It is essential that the commission does not stray from this matter, and instead has the capacity to articulate actions with the governments of Portugal and the governments of Germany and the Länder of Germany.

It is essential to bear in mind that this is an industry, as has already been said here, which is of interest to Europe, for its quality and value, for the research that develops it, for the environmental quality also associated with it. And it is essential that the Commission does not deviate.
I endorse the invitation made by my colleague Peneda for the Commissioner and the Commission to travel to Portugal.

It is essential that the Commission bears in mind that at this point, Europeans are looking to Europe. And they expect answers from the European authorities. They need to feel that the European authorities are close.

European citizens do not understand a Europe that washes its hands. Instead, they want a Europe that has the audacity to get its hands dirty and to get to work, helping them overcome difficulties. Thank you very much.

Mr. President, thank you very much. I also add my words to the tributes to Jan Mouni, but I asked to speak to commend the president for the statement he made regarding the European Day in memory of the victims of terrorism.
It was at my proposal that five years ago we adopted this orientation, which the council enshrined at a meeting on March 25th, following the tragic Madrid attacks.
But my intervention was an appeal.

The parliament has always been committed to the celebration of this date, but unfortunately, this date still hasn't had, within the European institutions and member states, the prominence it should have.
I believe that the celebration of this date is one of the most important instruments we have to, ah, honor the victims, as the president did, but also to raise a level of public awareness.
I know there are some celebrations in Madrid today, but little else.
And therefore, the appeal I make to the commission, to the Czech presidency, is that all member states duly celebrate this date in the future. Thank you very much.
Ladies and gentlemen, my dear colleagues, the crisis is deeper than we expected. And unemployment will be greater than we predict.
There are good reasons to suppose that the foreseen European stimulus is not sufficient. But it is clear, even now, that it is taking too long to reach the citizens.
The position of Parliament is strong and clear. It has been, and I hope it will continue to be so.

Our objective is to maintain employment.
And it is to create new jobs.
With cohesion, with solidarity, whether territorial or social.
And in this moment of crisis, citizens cannot accept a Europe that gives them no answers. A Europe that is powerless in the face of the problems they are experiencing.
What will the parliament ask the commission?

It naturally requests, through these reports, that you coordinate national interventions. It asks you to use all the means that are currently at your disposal to intervene, and to provide you with all the conditions as a budgetary entity for that to happen.

Asks you to launch a clear European pro-employment initiative. And tells you that it is important to have an agenda with a calendar to implement the measures for regulating the financial market, as well as to bring credit to the real economy.
But what is asked of the council?
requests its council to, above all, rekindle the political will that is at the essence of the construction of the European project.
The European Union is competition, but it is equally cohesion and solidarity.
And there cannot be a single market without this guarantee: solidarity and cohesion.
It was for this that we all delegated, at the European level, the national autonomies we had before joining this project. Thank you very much.

Madam President, Madam Commissioner,

The indicators continuously published internationally do not cease to confirm it.
And to that extent, it is absolutely disturbing the notion that simple gestures and practices not even very elaborate, nor particularly costly, could be sufficient to save many lives.

European financial support can be crucial in this regard. And we must always bear in mind how cooperation in the health sector is truly strategic. And it directly impacts not only one of the Millennium Development Goals, but many of the Millennium Development Goals.

Mr. Commissioner, never before today has the European project, built with so much effort over the last 50 years, been so threatened.

The frustration of European citizens is enormous. The financial system that should have served them destroyed their savings, their pensions, their investment projects, their jobs, in sum, their future.

Regarding the necessary strengthening of the entire financial services market — banking, insurance, securities — the path forward is clear. And it lies in the architecture that we are discussing here today.
We do indeed need a European supervisory architecture, strong, that covers all products and actors, that is equipped with methodologies and instruments
intervention and effective, and that is capable of responding to the reality of the market, which, especially in the European context, does not recognize national borders.

The risk cannot and should not be eliminated from the financial market. But we need regulation that makes this risk more transparent and prevents the accumulation of bubbles, whose explosion affects the entire system and spreads to the real economy.

But if, despite these defenses, new crises occur in the future, it is crucial to have mechanisms that limit contagion, especially concerning the banking system. Because this is vital for the functioning of the economy and is the depository of citizens' savings.

Here, precisely within the powers and competencies of the banking authority, lies the capacity to execute a new strategy for the management of cross-border crises in the banking sector. A topic to which my report refers.
It is not in our hands to prevent the bankruptcy of banks, but rather to ensure that their reorganization or their eventual liquidation are carried out
in an orderly manner, limiting the collateral effects on the rest of the system, and above all, avoiding that the taxpayer pays the costs of the crises they generate.

For this, a set of new devices is necessary. It is necessary to adopt common rules, definitions, methodologies, instruments, and terminology for all participants in restructuring, reorganization, or liquidation operations.
It is necessary to harmonize the relevant national legal frameworks for crisis management, namely the laws on bankruptcy and liquidation.
It is necessary to grant broad intervention powers to the supervisors.
It is necessary to design clear criteria, not only quantitative but also qualitative, that allow for the initiation of interventions in an institution facing difficulties.
It is necessary to prepare contingency plans.
This entire process, for 27 member states, requires time.
And meanwhile, around 50 banks, out of the 12,000 operating in Europe, represent 70% of the assets.
Well, this means a huge concentration of risk, but also an opportunity to intervene on them as a priority.

The conditions for us to propose a special and urgent process for the regulation of banks, which, being cross-border, due to their size, their relationships with other banks, have a possible systemic impact on the financial system.
Now, for these banks, it is possible to expedite a special supervision by the new European banking authority, although operating within the college of national supervisors.
It is possible and urgent that they, due to the risk they generate, start making contributions to a fund that finances eventual interventions.
that are necessary, and it is important that the banking authority creates a specialized unit that coordinates interventions in case of difficulty in these banks.
As a whole, the proposed elements ensure that interventions in transnational banks of a systemic nature are consistent and coherent, and among
different countries, and above all, that a principle so cherished in Europe is upheld: that the costs of pollution caused by these banks are borne by the polluter itself.

Let's take an action that is urgent and necessary to relaunch the hope of Europeans in Europe, in its financial system, and in particular in the banking system. Thank you very much.

Deforestation, as we know, is one of the main environmental and social problems on the planet. And each year we lose 13 million hectares of forest, thus contributing to 20% of global carbon emissions, and posing a huge threat to biodiversity.

The contribution of deforestation to climate change is undeniable, and, as such, it is largely responsible for the occurrence of extreme climatic events and floods, or even for the desertification of vast areas of the planet.
Forest loss puts all of humanity at risk. But this risk and this impact is not equally shared by all of us.
The communities with fewer resources and the communities that depend most on the forest and nature to survive, are the most affected.
Deforestation concerns our collective existence, the scarcity of natural resources, their distribution, the rights of local and indigenous communities, and social justice.
The main cause of deforestation is the illegal exploitation of timber, which represents 20 to 40% of the world's production of industrial timber.
Therefore, we are also talking about the transfer of wealth from the producing countries to the consuming countries of that same wood.
This illegal practice puts at risk the sustainable harvesting of timber and the companies that do it.
Pretty words are not enough.
This parliament must adopt strong and consequential measures in this fight.
This report takes a very significant step in that direction, by defining the prohibition of timber trade.
In some, there are also good intentions.
Effective measures are necessary, namely sanctions which in this case could be of three types.

First, fines that effectively remove the economic benefit from the illegal practice, aggravated by recidivism. Secondly, confiscation of the wood of the products in question, and thirdly, immediate suspension of the authorization to carry out a commercial activity.

And so, it is because anyone who exploits or commercializes a natural resource must be socially responsible. And so it is also that whoever places timber or timber products for the first time on the European Union market must be subject to due diligence to ensure that it is legally harvested timber.
That is why all subsequent operators must provide basic information about this wood, namely its provenance and its destination.
The population must still have the right, and I conclude here, Mr. President, the right to access information about who complies and who does not comply. Thank you very much.

Mr. President, the fact that there is a growing tendency for the safety profile of new medicines to only become fully known after their entry into the market, imposes the need for a pharmacovigilance system that is more effective than it has been until now.

New medications have played a fundamental role in public health and in advances in healthcare over the last decades.

The proposals presented reinforce the contribution of medicines to modern public health through a community pharmacovigilance system, improving coordination and transparency.
Its objective is monitoring, early detection, communication, and evaluation of adverse reactions.
The new medicines will have a graphic symbol that mentions they are still under scrutiny.

Thank you, Mr. President, Mr. Commissioner. A Europe without economic, social and territorial cohesion is not a union.
The mountainous regions, islands, and areas with low population density have their own, quite difficult challenges that must be overcome in the name of a Europe of the people.
But we must go beyond declarations of intent, otherwise, we will have the opposite effect, that is, we will reinforce inequalities.
These regions are enormous reservoirs of important resources.
Biodiversity, the forest, the wetlands, and the green areas provide an essential service for everyone, and yet, they suffer from the consequent limitations to their economy.
One must reciprocate.
The populations of these regions must be collectively supported by all.
I present two proposals.
First, the potential contribution to science and to development.
Many of these regions are authentic open-air laboratories, given their richness, but they do not have equal access to resources for research.
Why not then equip them with centers of research excellence, generating quality employment and boosting their economies?
A second proposal has to do with energy.
These regions can and should be project embryos that demonstrate the potential for self-sufficiency and energy sustainability, specifically through micro-generation from local sources.
Dependency was reduced, equality was increased, and job creation.
Lastly, I would like to state that using GDP as the sole or main indicator for the allocation of funds and aid can only result in more injustice.
Therefore, it is very important to use the supplementary data, namely those that portray the social and economic reality of these regions.
I cannot fail to make a special reference here to the people of the Azores, Madeira, the mountainous regions, and the areas of the depopulated Portuguese interior.
with their effort, greatly contribute to the development and quality of life of all Europeans.

I consider, for all this, this resolution as an important, but first step. Thank you very much.

Thank you, Mr. President. Mr. Commissioner, the objective of territorial cohesion, now enshrined in the Treaty of Lisbon, materializes the value of European solidarity and the commitment of the Member States and the Union to reduce existing disparities among the various regions.
However, it is not enough to express in the text of the treaties the objectives to be achieved.
There are regions of the Union that have permanent constraints, which strongly condition their economic and social development.

It is therefore necessary to implement specific programs that allow to effectively and truly overcome the backwardness of these regions, by promoting an adaptation of their development model to their potential and their resources, and contribute to achieving the objectives of the EU 2020 strategy.
This incentive to these regions should result not only from the means of regional policy, through its structural adjustment instruments, such as the Cohesion Fund and the four structural funds,
the extent of a different utilization of the various sectoral policies that have a significant territorial impact in each region and can, therefore, have a revitalizing effect on their economies.
For the determination of European assistance to these regions, it is certain that we must maintain GDP as the main indicator of eligibility.

However, and taking into account its natural disadvantages, it is pertinent to resort to other measurable criteria, capable of providing a more realistic picture of its level of development, such as the unemployment rate, population density, or the level of education.
Only in this way will it be possible to have a more complete picture of the complex reality of the most disadvantaged regions.

Remember, for example, the case of the outermost regions, whose status is recognized by the new treaty, and which are a group of regions in the European Union that have specific characteristics and therefore deserve special treatment.
The outermost regions, united by their particular characteristics, also diverge in their levels of development.

Also, regarding these, complementary GDP indicators should be used, capable of providing a more complete description of its specific reality, and thus contributing to a more accurate determination of its level of development. Thank you very much.

Mr. President, Commissioners, dear colleagues. After four consecutive years of reduction in the unemployment rate, from 2004 to 2008, the current economic crisis has nullified much of the progress made.
Unemployment rates in the European Union suffered an enormous increase. For example, in Portugal, we unfortunately reached a record value of an unemployment rate of 11.
Another negative phenomenon is youth unemployment in the European Union.
Europe is thus going through a period of transformation and faces enormous challenges.
The crisis annulled years of economic and social progress and exposed the fragilities of the economies of its member states and, consequently, of the European Union itself.
The resulting strategy outlines the paths to follow.
The European Union must intensify all its efforts to promote employment and social inclusion, as an integral part of its strategy to combat the economic and social crisis.
Thus, the proposed resolution on the future of the social fund currently under discussion is welcomed, as this fund plays a crucial role in achieving the objectives of the 2020 strategy.

In fact, this fund is the main financial instrument that allows the European Union to achieve the strategic objectives of its employment policy, improving the levels of education and qualification of its citizens.
The ESF must be strengthened as the main engine of the 2020 strategy, and must be used by member states to invest in qualification, in employment, in in training and in professional retraining.
Finally, I would like to stress that the reform of the European Social Fund, to be carried out in conjunction with the remaining structural funds, should facilitate and simplify its management and
reinforce transparency, as well as concentrating funds on obtaining results and realizing objectives that are effectively felt by citizens in their daily lives. Thank you very much.
The European Social Fund is probably the most structuring instrument of cohesion.

This is largely due to the prospective planning strategy used for employment, defining the factors on which to act, how to achieve the desired resource, and also to the use of the European Social Fund as an operational instrument of this strategy, acting on education, training, and employment promotion.
the horizontality of cohesion policy is the only way to ensure the success of the strategy.

GDP should continue to be the main eligibility criterion, but we must take into account other indicators that can provide a more complete picture of the regional reality.

The present directive, which recasts the previous directive that is over 10 years old, aims, as was recognized here by all, to put an end to a widespread problem in the European Union: late payments.
Delays, which in some member states are unjustifiably long when they involve public administration.

The negative effects of this practice on economic life are enormous. Both at the level of small and medium-sized enterprises and at the level of the internal market. This legislative amendment gives coherence and gives meaning to the European economic recovery plan and to the application of the small
business act. Certainly, this directive will improve companies' liquidity.
The creditors will now have an effective and efficient instrument to exercise their rights against bad debtors.
With this directive, the reprehensible practice of public entities will be the exception.

Now, it will be up to the institutions involved, to the Council and to the Member States, to allow the transposition of this directive to be carried out as quickly as possible.

And the second has to do with the fact that the weight of some large multinationals allows them to make prices work in their favor.
And the example of cereals is at this very clear level. It is dominated by six large multinationals.

First of all, one must ask why review VAT in the European Union.
First, there is a need to make it compatible with the single market.
The more VAT regimes and varieties, the less fluid the market will be.
A VAT reform favors e-commerce. It centers competition on the value of goods and the base price of goods and services, and not on tax differences.
It combats fraud and ensures much-needed confidence, especially in cross-border transactions. It improves the economy of SMEs and reduces administrative burdens.
What other complementary measures are already underway in the single market that can be linked to VAT?
Firstly, a simple, clear and transparent digital support mechanism, measure 7 of the single market.
Measure 5, uh, regarding services, the need to review the legislation, regarding standardization, and the implementation of the directive.

The new measure which aims to remove tax barriers and to explicitly review the VAT system. And the equalization of VAT in online commerce and in face-to-face commerce, which we all feel when we order books.
The pressures for VAT reform have two fundamental types of reasons.
First of all, the economic crisis.
More revenue is needed, including, probably in some countries, a reduction in labor contributions to promote economic development, shifting that burden to VAT.

Secondly, the second major source of pressure is population aging, demographic aging. And the tendency for indirect taxes to become direct taxes, which is due to the reduction in the population tax base.
It is necessary to rethink the balance between this direct and indirect taxation.
Finally, in these reforms, we have to reconcile subsidiarity with harmonization.
It is necessary to restrict the range of variation in the standard VAT rate so that we can all better understand each other.
Greater transparency is needed in the definition of the reduced VAT rate and its scope of application, a clear and widely publicised list of the reduced rates.
It is necessary to study exemption thresholds for SMEs, knowing full well that each exemption brings with it a trail of small or large frauds.
Finally, to welcome the Commission's recent proposal to create a European VAT.

This demonstrates the importance and the interest generated by this topic of new skills and jobs.
Ultimately, the goal is to prepare Europe's human resources, anticipating the needs of the labor market in the next decade.
The key strategy for preparing the report was to reformulate common ideas to avoid repetitions and ensure the report's coherence.
I think that objective was achieved.
We all know that the crisis currently affecting us has highlighted the fragilities of the European economic and social model, as well as nullified many of the achievements of the past.
Since the first effects of the crisis began to be felt in the labor markets in 2008, Europe has already lost more than 5 million jobs.
Today, almost 23 million people are unemployed.
Youth unemployment is even more alarming. The youth unemployment rate is close to 21% and represents more than double the general unemployment rate.
On the other hand, in all member states, unemployment rates vary with qualification levels.
This means that people with higher qualifications find a job more easily.
We also know that in the near future, the needs in terms of aptitudes, competencies, and qualifications will increase across all types and levels of profession.
In this context, Europe faces four challenges. First, the creation of jobs. Second, the need to develop, to remain competitive.
then the sustainability of social security systems. And finally, the difficulty in finding people with the appropriate skills to fill job offers.

This report thus has a dual objective. First, to highlight the need to act quickly to address the skills gaps in Europe. Second objective, to better anticipate the needs of the labour market in terms of skills.

For this, several measures stand out. First, to ensure the availability of skilled labor, it is necessary to improve basic competencies, as well as competencies in sciences, innovation, information and communication technologies, as well as in foreign languages.
As I have already mentioned, competency needs should be identified early, with a horizon of at least 10 years.
Today, we know the potential of a new sustainable economy, of the green economy, and the need for investment in ecological qualifications.
after that, participation in lifelong learning and professional requalification must be increased, as well as the reduction of early school leaving.
Another important matter is the mutual recognition of diplomas and professional qualifications, and the urgency of advancing with this recognition.
Fourthly, we draw attention to the connection that must exist between educational establishments and the needs of the labor market.
Internships should be of quality and supervised by tutors, in order to lead to employment and end any potential abuses of the intern situation.
Particular emphasis is also given to the role of social partners and to social dialogue.
The report also proposes measures to promote demand and job creation.
We have a future ahead of us, and we have to create jobs.

Having a global perspective and an integrated approach implies the interaction of European policies related to the sea, and calls for better governance through greater involvement in the European decision-making process.

The integrated maritime policy must, for that very reason, continue to benefit from adequate funding during the coming years of the current financial programming period.

Furthermore, the outermost regions contribute to the European Union having the most extensive exclusive economic zone in the world.

The Outermost Regions represent more than half of the European Union's exclusive economic zone, about 15 out of 25 million square kilometers, being, along with the coastal regions, fundamental for the economic growth of the European Union and its member states.

Our citizens will start to benefit from a sharp decrease in prices concerning calls, SMS messages, and data.
If nowadays there are operators that charge 3 euros per megabyte, from July, the maximum price they will be able to charge is 70 cents per megabyte, with this value dropping to 20 cents in 2014.

Second benefit, higher quality of information for consumers.
As early as this summer, operators will be required to inform consumers of the prices charged when they travel outside the European Union.

Third advantage: the possibility of more operators entering the market, which means increased competition that will stimulate better services and better prices.

Finally, in the near future, the consumer will be able, if they so wish, to have two contracts. One for services in the national territory and another for services abroad, maintaining the same mobile number and having only one bill.
will also be equally granted to the consumer, to our citizen, the possibility to contract with any European operator that offers them the best price.

Thank you very much, Mr. President. European leaders continue to insist on fiscal discipline, and I ask, fiscal discipline? Then let's get to it. Because what exactly are we talking about when we talk about fiscal discipline?

In my understanding, budgetary discipline is respecting contracts with pensioners and not abandoning the State's commitments halfway. In my understanding, budgetary discipline is respecting the contracts of those who work and not inventing extraordinary taxes or subsidy cuts.

budgetary, is to respect commitments in health, in education, in culture, and not close services or shut down and cancel projects halfway. Discipline is to put the banks in order and not go taking from taxpayers to put in the hands of speculators to save their banks.
This is the budgetary discipline that I would like to see implemented in the European Union, and not continue with this market free-for-all.

If an example is needed, the budget being discussed in Portugal at the Troika's command is the perfect example. It violates all commitments that any state should have with its citizens, so as not to violate the agreements made behind closed doors, which are ruining Europe and its countries.

Mr. President,
Dear colleagues, Commissioner,
It is a privilege to present the report on the 20 concerns of citizens and businesses.
relating to the functioning of the single market, precisely in this debate with Commissioner Barnier, in which the 20th anniversary of this market is marked.

A first word to thank the colleagues from all political groups and in particular the opinion rapporteurs and the shadow rapporteurs for their very constructive contributions and their very positive attitude.
It was a pleasure working with everyone, and the report benefited greatly from our combined effort.

We are well aware and proud that over these 20 years, the single market has been a driver of great economic and employment opportunities in Europe, which transformed the lives of European citizens and European companies.
Traveling, residing, and working within the Union are freedoms accessible to all citizens of the 27 Member States.
For European consumers, the single market means more freedom of choice and lower prices.
For our companies, the single market means more business opportunities, with access to 500 million consumers, which means more economic growth and more job creation.
To illustrate the progress that the single market has made in Europe since its creation in 1992, until the beginning of the crisis in
2008, one only needs to consider the 2.7 million jobs that were created and the 2.13% of additional GDP growth.
Today, making a phone call or receiving a phone call when traveling in Europe is 13% cheaper than in 2005.
The freedom of movement of people finds its most eloquent expression in the Erasmus program.
More than 2.5 million students benefited from this European exchange program for education and mobility.
In the last five years, companies also benefited from a 25% cut in administrative charges due to the single market legislation.
But a question arises: is everything going well in the single market?
No. Not everything works well.
The economic and financial crisis that affects us has had negative repercussions on the functioning of this market.

Our citizens, in some countries, living with the drama of unemployment and being confronted with austerity measures, began to have a skeptical view of Europe and a negative view regarding the internal market.
People still do not know or do not sufficiently understand their rights, nor do they know where to find information or help.
On the other hand, many member states unjustifiably delay the transposition of European directives and legislation on the single market, thus hindering their full potential.

In certain areas of the daily life of citizens, things as simple as, for example, the reimbursement of medical expenses after a temporary stay in another country of the Union, the opening of a bank account by a student of the program

Erasmus, changing energy suppliers, transferring pension rights, recognition of qualifications, and registering a vehicle in another member state are still very complex, difficult, or burdensome acts.
It is important to address these problems.
That was my concern as rapporteur.
Therefore, the message written in the report is very clear. We need more political will, much more political will from the member states and the European institutions.
council, above all, council and commission, to govern better and jointly. It is necessary to regain the trust of people and businesses. And it is necessary to facilitate the exercise of their rights.

Finally, I take this opportunity to salute the European Commission, in the person of Commissioner Barnier, for the presentation of Act for the Single Market II, 12 priority actions for new growth, which marks, I am certain, a new stage for the future. Thank you very much.
Madam President, Commissioner Barnier, and dear colleague Regina Bastos, my congratulations on your already very successful efforts.
When Jacques Delors presided over the European Commission, the Union's single market was created in 1992.
Delors then synthesized the three basic principles of the single market: competition that stimulates, cooperation that strengthens, and solidarity that unites.
20 years later, the economic and financial crisis rekindled the threat of national protectionism, and it became clear that the internal market is not a completed work.

The celebration of these 20 years must be used for a relaunch of the single market, with the concern of placing the market at the service of the citizen and not the citizen at the service of the market.
For this, priorities must be defined. And I would like to speak about one particular priority.
It is essential to have a common policy to produce and distribute energy at prices affordable to the consumer. With a high level of energy efficiency,
through the European energy market, served by cross-border infrastructures, with the possibility of community leverage for investment that represents enhanced European value.
In the energy sector too, Madam President, the common market will be decided.
the greatest achievements of the European Union, which surely represents one of the strongest pillars of the development of the Union, of its member states, and of its regions.

Regions like the Azores need to continue increasing their competitiveness by improving their competitive conditions. But we will never be able to do that if we don't better resolve our accessibility problems in the area of transport.

The Gozes report, together with the report we are going to prepare on the Union's annual growth prospects, at the end of this year, and which will precede the Spring Council, are two essential pieces of the so-called European Semester.
This report addresses the recommendations made to each of the member countries by the Council, on a proposal from the Commission. And these recommendations fit, as has just been said, with
"six-pack" and the future "two-pack", uh, an extraordinarily strong body, uh, that strengthens European control over the economic and budgetary policy of the member states.

The balance between this control and autonomy and legitimacy at national level is not easy to find. And it has fallen to a level of imposition, especially of discipline, and ultimately, it can even lead to a serious package of sanctions.

Regarding TUPAC, I would like to briefly comment that it is important that the council, which is absent here today, gives a negotiating mandate to its negotiators, compatible with the ambition to quickly determine this package, this TUPAC.

But more European coordination, returning to the topic, means less control for countries over their own destiny. Which requires a subtle balance, but also an increased demand regarding the quality of the recommendations that are made to countries in at least three or four dimensions.

First. It is important to ensure consistency between medium and long-term objectives, which include growth, employment, and real convergence, and short-term recommendations, focused, above all, on discipline and nominal convergence. Secondly, there must be consistency

with the conclusions that must be drawn regarding the analysis of macroeconomic imbalances, in particular, regarding the asymmetric impact of common policies and the negative externalities, the spillover effects, of some national policies on other countries.

Thirdly, we need to agree that there is a minimum threshold of social, economic, and labor rights, which cannot be exceeded by these recommendations. And finally, it is important that the quality of the recommendations is assessed based on the results achieved, which is far from being the case so far.

It's true, and since it has been referred to many times, that there are many things we don't know about the extraction of shale gas.
But there are also many that we already know, and it's important not to forget them.

We know, for example, that each shale gas extraction operation uses more than 19 million liters of potable water, which is enough to supply, in one year, 1,000 European citizens in terms of their needs.

We also know that each shale gas extraction that is performed, in each of the operations, 30, 300, I apologize, tons are pumped into the soil. They can go up to 300 tons of chemicals, and about 80% of which remain in the soil and do not leave there.

We also know that in each shale gas extraction, radioactive elements and heavy metals are released into the subsoil, which, along with the evaporation of toxic waste resulting from the process, constitute an effective threat to public health.
This is a danger of contamination of drinking water resources, and it is a contamination that is real and imminent.
Knowing this, we also know that shale gas extraction is a seemingly easy response, which only considers the immediate, industry, and profit, ignoring the populations, the future, and the environment.

But this is also the Europe that knows, in this house, that water is an essential, vital, and important resource, that knows how to protect the health of citizens and that must protect it. That seeks to make agreements to avoid emissions and to propose energy efficiency because we know that environmental standards are important. Environmental standards and those of health.
Therefore, when not everything is known, being prudent is the minimum.
And they may even want to do it, but do not do it in our name, nor in the name of the citizens who are here for us to represent.
